questions.question: Explain the difference between overloading an operator as a member function versus as a friend function with respect to the number of parameters.
keyPoints: Member binary operator takes 1 parameter (right operand only)
Friend binary operator takes 2 parameters (both operands explicit)
Member unary operator takes 0 parameters
Friend unary operator takes 1 parameter

questions.question: Why is the 'this' pointer important in member operator functions?
keyPoints: Provides implicit access to the left operand
Reduces number of parameters needed
Enables object to operate on itself (for unary)
Maintains encapsulation without friend declaration

questions.question: List two operators that MUST be overloaded as member functions and explain why.
keyPoints: Assignment operator (=) - required for proper object assignment
Subscript operator ([]) - requires object identity for indexing
Function call operator () - must have object context
Member access (->) - requires object pointer semantics

questions.question: What are the advantages of using member operator functions?
keyPoints: Maintains encapsulation - can access private members
Left operand must be class type (controlled)
Cleaner syntax without friend keyword
'this' pointer provides implicit left operand

questions.question: Why should binary arithmetic operators return by reference?
keyPoints: Enables operator chaining like (a + b) + c
Avoids unnecessary copy operations
Allows result to be used as lvalue
Maintains efficiency for complex objects

questions.question: Explain one limitation of member operator functions compared to friend operators.
keyPoints: Cannot apply implicit type conversion to left operand
Left operand must always be class type (cannot be built-in)
Cannot be used for symmetric operations easily
May require more member functions for different types

questions.question: Write the general syntax for overloading a binary operator as a member function.
keyPoints: ReturnType operatorop(const ClassName& operand)
Uses 'this' pointer for left operand
Body accesses members via this->member or direct member name
Returns appropriate type (usually reference for chaining)
